turns.content: Imagine I'm building a microcontroller firmware that uses the alloc crate but I want to store some data on the heap using Box or Vec without relying on std::alloc. Is it actually possible to get a real heap there, or am I just simulating pointers?

You are encountering a fundamental misunderstanding about how memory works in no_std environments. You cannot simply call #[global_allocator] and expect a standard heap like you would in std mode because the runtime environment is different. In no_std, the alloc crate provides an interface to manage memory but it does not create a global allocator on its own; it requires you to specify one via #[global_allocator]. Without that macro, functions like Box::new or Vec::with_capacity will panic at runtime because they try to access memory management traits that do not exist. You need to implement the GlobalAlloc trait yourself for your embedded system using something like embedded-alloc, which provides a minimal allocator suitable for constrained environments.
Okay so I have to write my own allocator then? That sounds tedious and why would anyone ever use the alloc crate if it just forces you to do this?

The allocation process is not that tedious because crates like embedded-alloc already implement the GlobalAlloc trait for common systems. You can reuse their implementations instead of writing from scratch. The reason people use the alloc crate in no_std contexts is because it unifies memory management across different environments, allowing you to write code that works with both standard and embedded targets without changing your logic. It provides a consistent interface so you don't have to worry about the underlying details of how the heap is managed.
So if I'm using an allocator that isn't std::alloc, then Box and Vec still work but they use my custom allocator instead? And what happens when memory runs out?

Yes, you can use Box and Vec in no_std environments as long as your environment implements the GlobalAlloc trait. When those types allocate memory, they call into your allocator's methods like alloc() and dealloc(). If your embedded system has very limited resources or cannot handle reallocation, then a panic will occur if all available memory is exhausted because there is no fallback mechanism to swap out blocks of memory.
I see. So in std mode the runtime handles re-allocation automatically but in no_std I have to be careful about lifetime? And what about String? Does it behave differently?

In std environments, the standard library manages internal buffers and re-allocates memory as needed, so you rarely notice when a string grows. In no_std, each allocation is explicit and your allocator must handle these requests directly. The behavior of String remains similar to other heap types because it still uses the same underlying allocation logic provided by GlobalAlloc. However, in embedded systems where the heap is very small, you might choose to use stack-allocated strings or fixed-size buffers instead to avoid dynamic allocation entirely.
That makes sense. So I should probably look at embedded-alloc and see how it defines the allocator trait for my specific chip? And can I mix std types with no_std code?

You should definitely check out embedded-alloc to understand how to configure your allocator for your specific hardware constraints. Mixing standard library types from std with no_std code is generally not possible because they rely on different runtime assumptions about memory management and panic behavior, so you would need to implement a custom wrapper or use crates that bridge these gaps if you want to share some common logic.

I'm trying to port a big C project into Rust for an embedded device, but I keep running into issues with memory. When the docs say we can't use standard library things like Vec or String in no_std environments, it sounds like everything gets wiped out before the program even starts.

That's a common misconception. You don't lose all data; rather, you lose the automatic global memory allocator that std provides. In a no_std context, the runtime doesn't know where your heap is, so if you try to allocate a Vec without setting up a custom allocator first, it panics immediately because there's nothing to use for the Box or String allocation.
Oh I see. So does that mean I have to write my own memory management functions like realloc and free from scratch?

You don't need to implement the logic yourself, but you do need to provide an implementation for the GlobalAlloc trait so your crate can see where heap space exists. Think of it like this: the standard allocator is just a default box that happens to live on the OS stack or malloc region; in no_std, you are building your own global allocator which acts as the interface between Rust's type system and whatever physical memory your microcontroller uses.
Okay so I should import some crate that implements GlobalAlloc instead of just using alloc::Vec directly?

Exactly. You need to declare #[global_allocator] in your main module and then use the embedded-alloc crate or similar to get a working allocator. Once you have that, you can safely instantiate Vec, Box, and String because they will delegate their allocation requests through your custom GlobalAlloc implementation rather than trying to call free() on nothing.
Wait so the difference is just that instead of malloc we are calling something else?

Not exactly another function, but yes conceptually similar. The standard library's Vec uses malloc internally which returns a pointer to heap memory; your custom allocator does the same thing but operates on whatever RAM layout your device has. It provides the same safety guarantees for Box and String creation without requiring an OS-level C runtime.
That makes sense. So if I'm using embedded-alloc, do I need to worry about fragmentation or anything?

You should still consider memory layout carefully since embedded devices have limited RAM. The allocator itself handles fragmentation differently than a C malloc might, but you can configure it to use fixed-size buffers or buddy allocation depending on your needs. Just keep in mind that unlike std::alloc which manages arbitrary sizes, embedded allocators often trade some flexibility for predictability.
Alright so my strategy is: set up the global allocator first, then drop down to no_std and use Vec normally?

That's exactly right. Once your GlobalAlloc is declared and linked, you can treat Vec, Box, and String as normal types throughout your codebase. The only caveat is that if you ever need to debug memory usage in a real-time system, you might want to add some instrumentation around the allocator calls.

I'm getting this error when trying to use Box::new() on my embedded board: 'global allocator not found'. I just followed the exercise from the book where they tell me to replace std with core. Why can't I even allocate a single Box anymore? I thought I knew how to make structs work without the standard library, but this feels like it should be possible.

The error happens because in no_std environments there is no global allocator configured by default, so when you try to create a Box or push into a Vec, the runtime panics immediately. You cannot use Box::new() or allocate memory for String without defining your own allocator first using #[global_allocator]. The alloc crate provides a standard heap implementation that works alongside core, but on bare metal you need embedded-alloc which mimics std's behavior while managing its own free list. Your code isn't wrong; the environment just lacks the infrastructure to handle dynamic allocation.

Okay so I should just add the alloc crate? But then why does the book mention embedded-alloc instead? Is that actually better than using the standard allocator from the alloc crate? I'm frustrated because I spent twenty minutes trying to fix this and now you're telling me I can't even use Box anymore. This is supposed to be an easy exercise!

You are right to feel frustrated, but the distinction matters for your specific hardware. The standard allocator from alloc crate works well for most microcontrollers with enough RAM, but embedded-alloc is designed specifically for systems where memory is extremely constrained or fragmented. It tracks every allocation and deallocation manually using a free list instead of relying on malloc-like behavior. If you choose embedded-alloc over the default one, your code will be more predictable in terms of memory usage and you won't accidentally leak pages that are hard to reclaim later.

I get it now about the free list, but I still can't figure out how to make my Vec<String> work with embedded-alloc. The book says just replace std::vec with something else? What exactly do I need to change in my code and which traits must be implemented?

To make Vec<String> work you need to implement the alloc::alloc trait for your custom allocator type, then set it as #[global_allocator] before defining any heap types like String or Box. You should also ensure your struct implements the necessary layout traits so that embedded-alloc can correctly calculate memory requirements. Once the global allocator is defined and linked with core, you can safely use Vec<String> just like normal Rust code.

I'm reviewing a new microcontroller project that runs pure no_std code, but the existing team is using Box and Vec with the default allocator. I noticed they're getting heap panics at runtime when memory gets tight. Why does this happen in no_std environments?

The panic happens because your current implementation relies on a global static allocation that isn't available in bare-metal contexts. In standard Rust, the heap is managed by a GlobalAlloc trait implementation provided by the std library, but in no_std you must provide your own #[global_allocator] before using alloc crate types like Box or Vec. Without this custom allocator registered, attempts to allocate memory will either fail silently or cause immediate panics depending on your panic hook configuration. The specific issue is that the default allocator implementation assumes a global heap exists, which doesn't exist in embedded systems unless you explicitly define one.
So I need to write my own #[global_allocator] and register it with alloc? But what if we want to use some standard library crates that depend on the default heap?

You cannot safely use standard library crates like std::vec::Vec or Box without first establishing a proper allocator. Even if you try to link against libc or other C libraries, they expect a specific memory layout and allocation strategy that no_std code won't provide by default. The only way forward is to either implement your own #[global_allocator] using crate: embedded-alloc or similar solutions designed for microcontrollers, or restrict the scope of your application to avoid using any heap types until you have an allocator in place.
I see, so we can't just drop down to no_std and keep using existing Vec implementations. What happens if I try to use Box specifically?

Using Box without a custom #[global_allocator] will cause immediate runtime failure because Box requires dynamic allocation through the GlobalAlloc trait. When your code tries to create a Box, it calls into the allocator, which in a bare-metal context with no registered implementation simply cannot fulfill that request. This isn't just an optimization issue; it's a fundamental breaking of assumptions about memory management that only standard library types make.
Got it. But I'm reading code that uses embedded-alloc and seems to work fine. What makes that different?

The difference is that embedded-alloc provides a custom #[global_allocator] implementation specifically designed for microcontroller memory constraints, often mapping directly to the hardware's RAM or SRAM regions. This allocator knows how to allocate and deallocate memory in ways standard Rust allocators don't, such as using ring buffers or specialized data structures that minimize fragmentation. Your existing code is failing because it assumes a global heap exists, which embedded-alloc creates through its custom implementation.
That makes sense. Is there any way to migrate our current codebase gradually?

Gradual migration isn't really possible without replacing the allocator first. You cannot incrementally add no_std support because every use of Box, Vec, or String requires a functioning GlobalAlloc implementation before it's safe to compile. The only viable path is to identify all heap-dependent types in your codebase and either remove them or wrap them with smart pointers that can operate without direct memory allocation until you've implemented the proper #[global_allocator] for your specific hardware.
So if I add embedded-alloc now, will everything just work?

Adding embedded-alloc alone won't make everything work immediately. You need to ensure your Cargo.toml includes the crate and that you've placed #[global_allocator] in the correct location before compilation. Additionally, some crates might still depend on std features internally even if your main crate is no_std, so you may need to audit dependencies for hidden std usage. The real fix requires replacing every instance of Box or Vec with heap-free alternatives like Rc, Arc, or owned data structures that don't require dynamic allocation.
One last question: what about String? Can we use it anywhere?

You cannot use String in pure no_std environments without a custom allocator because String requires dynamic memory management. Even if you implement embedded-alloc, using String directly will still trigger allocations that your hardware might not support. The solution is to replace String with owned byte arrays or use smart pointers like Rc<[u8; N]> where N is known at compile time, which avoids the need for heap allocation entirely.
